question_body: I am trying to implement a plugin system where I have a base class 'Plugin' and several derived classes. I store these in a std::vector<Plugin*>. When I iterate through the vector, how can I determine if a specific pointer is pointing to a 'AudioPlugin' or a 'VideoPlugin' to call specific methods?

Suppose I have a class hierarchy where 'Shape' is the parent and 'Circle' and 'Square' are children. If I have a pointer of type Shape*, is there a way to identify the actual derived type of the object at runtime without manually adding an enum ID to the base class?

I'm working with a library that uses a base class for all its components. I've created my own derived class, but the library's API only accepts the base pointer. How can the library internal code distinguish my custom class from the standard derived classes provided by the library?
In C++, if I have a base class pointer pointing to an object of a derived class, what is the standard way to check the exact type of the derived class before performing a cast?

I have a base class 'Entity' and derived classes 'Player' and 'Enemy'. I have a list of Entity pointers. I need to execute different logic depending on whether the entity is a Player or an Enemy. How do I detect the derived type?
Is there a way to identify the class of an object when it is being accessed via a base class pointer, specifically when the derived class is defined by the end-user of my framework?

I am implementing a system where users inherit from my 'BaseComponent' class. I need to know if the object is a direct child of 'BaseComponent' or a child of 'AdvancedComponent' (which also inherits from BaseComponent). How can I achieve this?
When using polymorphism, how can I verify if a base class pointer is actually pointing to a specific derived class instance?
I have a base class and multiple levels of inheritance. If I hold a pointer to the top-level base class, how can I check if the object belongs to a specific leaf class in the inheritance tree?
How can I detect the runtime type of an object when it's passed as a base class reference to a function, especially if the derived classes are defined in different modules?
If I have 'class A' and 'class B : public A', and I have a pointer 'A* ptr', how can I programmatically check if 'ptr' is actually an instance of 'B'?

I am designing a GUI framework. All widgets inherit from 'Widget'. I need a way to check if a 'Widget*' pointer is actually a 'Button' or a 'Slider' to apply specific styling. What is the best approach?
Can I use RTTI to determine the specific derived class of an object when I only have access to its base class pointer?
I have a base class 'Animal' and derived classes 'Dog' and 'Cat'. I have a pointer of type Animal*. How can I differentiate between the Dog and Cat instances at runtime?
Is it possible to check if a user-defined class inherits from a specific intermediate class when the object is passed as a pointer to a root base class?
How do I implement a type-check mechanism in C++ to find out which derived class a base class pointer is currently referencing?
I'm building a game engine where all game objects inherit from 'GameObject'. I need to check if a pointer is specifically a 'PhysicsObject' to access its velocity properties. How is this done?
Given a base class pointer, how can I determine if the object it points to was instantiated from a specific user-defined derived class?
What is the difference between using a type ID member variable and using dynamic_cast to check the derived type of a base class pointer?
I have a hierarchy: Base -> Mid -> Derived. If I have a Base* pointer, how can I check if the object is specifically of type Mid or specifically of type Derived?
In a scenario where I have a base class pointer, how can I safely identify the derived type to ensure I am calling the correct specialized methods?
How can I determine if an object is an instance of a particular derived class when the object is hidden behind a base class interface?
I need to distinguish between different derived classes when they are stored in a container of base class pointers. What is the most efficient way to do this?
Is there a way to check the inheritance chain of an object at runtime using only a pointer to its base class?
If I provide a base class in my library, how can I tell if the user has derived their class from my base class or from one of my provided intermediate classes?
How can I use the 'typeid' operator to compare a base class pointer against a specific derived class type?
I have a base class 'Document' and derived classes 'PDFDocument' and 'WordDocument'. How do I check which one a 'Document*' pointer is pointing to?
When working with a base class pointer, how can I confirm if the actual object is a derived class created by the user?
What is the recommended way to identify the specific subclass of a base pointer in a polymorphic class hierarchy?
I have a pointer to a base class. I want to know if the object is of a specific derived type before I cast it. How can I achieve this safely?
If I have multiple classes inheriting from a single base class, how can I differentiate them when they are passed as base class pointers to a function?
How can I check if a pointer to a base class is pointing to an object of a specific derived class in a system with multiple inheritance?
I'm creating a framework where users inherit from 'Base'. I need to detect if the user-defined class also inherits from 'InterfaceA' or 'InterfaceB'. How can I do this via the Base pointer?
Is there a way to reflect the actual class name of a derived object when accessing it through a base class pointer?
How can I implement a check to see if a base class pointer is pointing to a specific subclass without using a manual type-flag?
I have a base class 'Command' and several derived command classes. How can I determine which specific command is being executed when I only have a 'Command*' pointer?
How do I check if an object is an instance of a particular derived class when the pointer is of the base class type and the class is polymorphic?
If I have a pointer to a base class, how can I verify if it points to a derived class that the user has implemented?
What is the best practice for checking the derived type of an object when using a base class pointer in a large-scale C++ project?
How can I detect if a base class pointer refers to a specific derived class in a hierarchy where there are several layers of inheritance?
I have a pointer to 'class Base'. I want to know if it's actually 'class DerivedA' or 'class DerivedB'. How can I do this without modifying the base class?
In C++, how can I identify the concrete class of an object when it's stored as a pointer to its abstract base class?
How can I check if a base class pointer is pointing to a derived class that inherits from a specific intermediate class provided by my library?
I have a list of pointers to a base class. I need to perform a different operation if the pointer is of a specific derived type. How can I implement this check?
Is there a way to determine the derived type of an object at runtime if I only have a reference to the base class?
How can I use dynamic_cast to check if a base class pointer is actually pointing to a specific derived class?
I have a base class 'Node' and derived classes 'LeafNode' and 'BranchNode'. How can I tell which one a 'Node*' pointer is pointing to?
If a user defines a class that inherits from my library's base class, how can I identify that specific derived class at runtime?
How can I verify the derived type of an object when it's passed as a pointer to a base class in a polymorphic hierarchy?
I have a base class pointer and I need to know if the object is an instance of a specific derived class to access its members. What is the correct way to do this?

I am struggling with a design decision regarding an inner class that needs to be tested. Currently, my inner class accesses private members of the outer class directly. To make it testable with JUnit and Mockito, I moved the inner class to its own top-level class and passed the required dependencies through the constructor. Some colleagues argue that I've broken the encapsulation of the outer class by exposing these dependencies to a separate class. Is it better to keep the class private/inner and use reflection to test it, or is the refactoring approach the industry standard for maintaining clean code?

In a Java project, I have a complex outer class with several private helper inner classes. I've found that the logic inside one of these inner classes is buggy. Because it's private and depends on the outer class's state, I cannot instantiate it in a test file. I'm considering changing the inner class to be package-private or public to facilitate unit testing. Does this violate the principle of encapsulation, or is 'testability' a valid reason to relax access modifiers?

I have a scenario where an inner class is performing heavy business logic and accessing private fields of the outer class. To implement unit tests, I extracted the inner class into a standalone class and used dependency injection via the constructor to provide the necessary state. This increased the amount of boilerplate code significantly. Am I sacrificing proper encapsulation for the sake of testability, or is this the correct way to decouple logic for testing purposes?

Is it a breach of encapsulation to use reflection to access private inner classes and their private fields during unit testing? I have a class where the core logic is hidden in a private inner class, and I want to verify its behavior without changing the visibility of the class or its members. Would refactoring the inner class into a separate public class with a constructor be considered 'over-engineering' if it only serves the purpose of testing?

I'm refactoring a legacy system where an inner class relies heavily on the private state of its outer class. To introduce unit tests, I'm thinking of moving the inner class to its own file and passing the outer class's dependencies as arguments. My concern is that this exposes internal implementation details that were previously hidden. Is there a way to maintain encapsulation while still achieving high test coverage for the logic previously contained in the inner class?

When dealing with private inner classes that contain complex logic, what is the preferred approach for unit testing? I've seen two patterns: one where the inner class is kept private and tested indirectly via the outer class, and another where it's promoted to a public class to be tested in isolation. Does the latter approach undermine encapsulation, and if so, is the trade-off worth the gain in test granularity?

I have a class `OrderProcessor` with a private inner class `TaxCalculator`. The `TaxCalculator` uses private fields from `OrderProcessor`. To write a unit test for `TaxCalculator`, I had to make it a public static class and pass the required data via a constructor. This means `OrderProcessor` now has to explicitly pass its internal state. Does this pattern break encapsulation, or is this simply the cost of making a system modular and testable?

I am debating whether to use a 'test-only' package-private visibility for an inner class to allow it to be tested in a separate test class. The alternative is to keep it private and use the outer class's public API to trigger the inner class's logic. The latter makes it hard to test edge cases. Does exposing the inner class for testing purposes violate the core tenets of encapsulation?

In a recent code review, a teammate suggested that by moving a private inner class to a separate file to make it testable, I have 'leaked' the internal state of the outer class. The inner class now requires a constructor that takes several of the outer class's private services. Is this an example of breaking encapsulation, or is the teammate confusing encapsulation with 'hiding things from the compiler'?

If I have a private inner class that is too complex to be tested through the public methods of the outer class, should I refactor it into a separate class? If I do, I'll need to pass in the outer class's private variables, which seems to contradict the goal of encapsulation. Is there a middle ground that allows for isolated unit testing without exposing the internal dependencies of the outer class?

I have a `Service` class with a private inner `Handler` class. The `Handler` accesses private members of `Service`. I want to write a unit test for `Handler` specifically. If I move `Handler` to its own file and pass the dependencies via a constructor, I feel like I'm adding unnecessary boilerplate and breaking the conceptual encapsulation of the `Service`. Is there a way to test the `Handler` without these changes?

Does promoting a private inner class to a public class solely for the purpose of unit testing constitute a violation of encapsulation? I find myself in a position where I must either leave the logic untested or expose it to the rest of the package. Which is the lesser of two evils in a production-grade codebase?

I'm trying to decide between using reflection to test a private inner class or refactoring that class into a public one. The refactoring requires adding a constructor to the new class to accept dependencies that were previously accessed implicitly. Does this increase the surface area of the API and thus break encapsulation, or does it actually improve the design by making dependencies explicit?

I have a logic-heavy private inner class that is currently impossible to unit test. I've decided to move it to a separate class and use a constructor to inject the dependencies it needs from the outer class. My lead developer says this 'breaks the encapsulation' of the original class. I disagree, as the dependencies are still private within the new class. Who is correct in this interpretation of encapsulation?

When a private inner class is refactored into a standalone class to facilitate mocking and unit testing, we often end up adding several parameters to the new class's constructor. Since these parameters are internal details of the original outer class, does this refactoring effectively destroy the encapsulation of the original component?

I have a complex `Manager` class with a private inner `Worker` class. To test `Worker`, I moved it to a separate file and passed the `Manager`'s private dependencies into the `Worker`'s constructor. Is this a valid application of the Single Responsibility Principle, or have I just broken the encapsulation of the `Manager` class by exposing its internals to the `Worker` class?

Is it a design smell to make a private inner class public just so it can be unit tested? I'm worried that I'm breaking encapsulation and that other developers might start using this class in ways I didn't intend. Is there a way to ensure the class remains 'effectively private' while still being accessible to a test suite?

I'm refactoring a class where a private inner class is doing most of the work. I want to use Mockito to mock the dependencies the inner class uses. To do this, I had to move the inner class to a separate file. Now the outer class has to pass its private fields into the inner class. Does this move from implicit access to explicit passing of state break the principle of encapsulation?

I have an inner class that relies on the outer class's private fields. I want to write a unit test for it. I've heard that using reflection to access private members for testing is a 'hack' and that refactoring to a separate class is 'correct'. However, the refactoring seems to expose internal state. Which approach is more aligned with the philosophy of encapsulation?

If I move a private inner class to its own file and use a constructor to inject the dependencies it needs, am I breaking the encapsulation of the outer class? The inner class was previously a 'secret' implementation detail, but now it's a visible class in the package. Does this change the architectural integrity of the system?

I have a case where an inner class is tightly coupled to the outer class. To test the inner class, I've converted it to a public class and added a constructor to pass in the dependencies. This has added about 20 lines of boilerplate. Is this an acceptable trade-off for testability, or does it violate the principle of encapsulation by making internal dependencies explicit?

In Java, if I want to test a private inner class without using reflection, I usually move it to a separate class. However, this requires me to pass the outer class's private state into the new class's constructor. Does this 'leak' the internal state of the outer class, thereby breaking encapsulation?

I'm dealing with a class that has a private inner class. The inner class has a bug, and I can't write a unit test for it because it's private. If I change it to public and pass in the dependencies, am I breaking encapsulation? Or should I just test it indirectly through the outer class's public methods, even if that makes the tests brittle and hard to write?

Is it a violation of encapsulation to move a private inner class to a separate file and use a constructor to provide it with the necessary dependencies from the outer class? I feel that this makes the code more verbose and exposes internal details that should remain hidden.

I have a private inner class that uses several private fields of the outer class. I want to test it in isolation. If I move it to a separate class and inject the dependencies, does that break encapsulation? Or is it better to keep it as an inner class and use a reflection-based utility to access it for testing?

I have a design where a private inner class handles a specific part of the logic. To make it testable, I've moved it to a separate class. Now the outer class must explicitly pass its private dependencies to the inner class. Does this change in visibility and dependency management break the principle of encapsulation?

I am refactoring a class to improve its testability. I've moved a private inner class to its own file and added a constructor to pass in the required services. Some say this is the only way to properly unit test, others say it breaks encapsulation. What is the consensus on this approach?

When an inner class is moved to a separate file to allow for unit testing, we often have to pass the outer class's private state into the new class. Does this 'exposure' of private state constitute a break in encapsulation, or is it simply a move toward a more decoupled architecture?

I have a private inner class that is very complex. To test it, I've made it a public class in a separate file and added a constructor to inject the dependencies. Is this a breach of encapsulation, or is it a necessary step for achieving a maintainable and testable codebase?

If I use reflection to test a private inner class, I maintain encapsulation but the tests are fragile. If I refactor the inner class to a separate public class, the tests are robust but I've exposed internal dependencies. Which approach is more 'correct' from an encapsulation perspective?

I have a private inner class that accesses private variables of the outer class. To write unit tests, I moved the inner class to its own file and passed those variables through the constructor. Does this approach break encapsulation by making the inner class's dependencies explicit and visible?

Is it acceptable to break encapsulation by making a private inner class public if the only reason is to enable unit testing? Or should the logic be tested through the public API of the outer class, even if that means the tests are less precise?

I've moved a private inner class to a separate file to facilitate testing with mocks. This required me to add a constructor to the new class to accept the outer class's private dependencies. Does this design change violate the principle of encapsulation?

I have a complex inner class that I need to unit test. I've decided to extract it into its own class and use constructor injection for its dependencies. Does this refactoring break the encapsulation of the original outer class, or does it actually improve the overall design?

When refactoring a private inner class into a separate class for testing, we often end up passing the outer class's private fields into the new class's constructor. Does this act of passing internal state break the encapsulation of the outer class?

I'm struggling with a decision: keep a logic-heavy inner class private and test it via the outer class (poor test coverage), or make it a separate class and pass in dependencies (potential encapsulation break). Which path is more aligned with professional software engineering standards?

I have a class with a private inner class that is difficult to test. I've moved the inner class to a separate file and passed the required dependencies through the constructor. Is this an example of breaking encapsulation, or is it simply making the dependencies explicit for the sake of testability?

Is it a breach of encapsulation to expose the internal state of a class to a separate class just so that the separate class can be unit tested? This happens often when extracting a private inner class into its own file.

I've extracted a private inner class into a standalone class to make it testable. This required adding a constructor to the new class to accept dependencies from the original outer class. Does this refactoring break the encapsulation of the outer class?

If I move a private inner class to a separate file and use a constructor to inject dependencies, am I breaking the principle of encapsulation? I'm worried that I'm making the internal workings of my class too visible.

I have a private inner class that is too complex to test through the outer class's public methods. I've refactored it into a separate class and used constructor injection. Does this approach violate encapsulation, or is it the recommended way to handle testability in Java?
Is using reflection to test a private inner class a better way to preserve encapsulation than refactoring that inner class into a separate, public class with constructor-injected dependencies?

I have a class where a private inner class performs the core logic. To test it, I've moved it to its own file and passed the outer class's private fields as arguments. Does this break the encapsulation of the outer class, or is it a valid architectural move?

When moving a private inner class to a separate file for testing, we must pass the required internal state via the constructor. Does this process of making internal dependencies explicit break the encapsulation of the original class?

I have a private inner class that is hard to test. I've moved it to a separate class and used dependency injection. Does this refactoring break encapsulation, or does it follow the principle of separating concerns to improve testability?

If I extract a private inner class to a separate file to allow for unit testing, I have to pass the outer class's private members into the new class. Does this violate the principle of encapsulation, or is it an acceptable trade-off for better test coverage?

I'm debating whether to keep a complex inner class private and use reflection for testing, or make it a separate class and pass in dependencies. Which approach better respects the principle of encapsulation while ensuring the code is fully tested?

I'm transitioning from C# to Java and I'm struggling with the concept of final fields. I thought that marking a variable as final meant the object it points to becomes immutable. However, I see this code where a final List is being modified using the .add() method. How is this possible if the reference is final? Does final only apply to the reference and not the internal state of the object?

Can someone explain the difference between a final reference and an immutable object in Java? I have a static final ArrayList in my class, and I can still add and remove elements from it. I thought final meant the value couldn't change. Is this a violation of encapsulation or just how Java handles references?

I am new to Java and trying to understand the 'final' keyword. If I declare a 'private static final Set' for a cache, why can I still put new items into that set? I was under the impression that final prevented any changes to the variable after initialization.

In my current project, I see a lot of 'private static final Map' being used for lookup tables. I'm confused because the map is being updated during runtime via a public method. If the map is final, shouldn't the compiler throw an error when we call .put()? Please clarify.

Coming from a background in embedded C, I'm confused by Java's final modifier. I see a class where a final Map is used to store configuration settings, and the settings are updated dynamically. Does 'final' in Java only prevent the reassignment of the variable itself, rather than the modification of the object's contents?

I'm trying to implement a singleton-style cache using a static final HashMap. I noticed that I can still add entries to the map throughout the application lifecycle. I thought final meant the object was constant. Is there a way to make the actual contents of the map final as well?

What is the actual meaning of 'static final' when applied to a Collection in Java? I'm seeing code where a final List is modified frequently, which contradicts my understanding that final variables cannot be changed after they are assigned.

I'm learning about encapsulation in Java. If I have a 'private static final Map' in a service class, can I still modify the values inside that map? If so, what is the purpose of marking it final if the data inside can still be altered?

I'm confused about the behavior of final objects in Java. I created a final HashMap to act as a constant, but I found that I can still call .put() and .clear() on it. Does final only prevent me from saying 'userMap = new HashMap()', but allows me to change the map's entries?

Could someone explain why a 'static final Map' allows the addition of new key-value pairs? I thought the final keyword made the variable immutable. Is there a difference between the reference being final and the object being immutable?

I am seeing a pattern in my codebase where 'private static final List' is used, yet the list is populated at runtime. I thought final meant the value was set in stone. Am I misunderstanding how Java handles final references to objects?

Does the 'final' keyword in Java provide any actual protection for the data inside a Collection? I have a final Map, but any method in my class can add or remove elements. I'm trying to understand if this is the intended behavior of encapsulation in Java.

I'm a C++ developer moving to Java. In C++, 'const' often means the object cannot be changed. In Java, I have a 'static final Map' that I can still modify. Is 'final' in Java equivalent to a constant pointer rather than a constant object?

I'm trying to wrap my head around Java's 'static final' keyword. If I define a map as static final, why can I still add elements to it using the put method? I thought this would be illegal once the object was initialized.

Is it correct to say that a 'final' Map in Java only prevents the map variable from being reassigned to a different Map object, but doesn't stop the map itself from being modified? I'm seeing this in a mock implementation and it's confusing me.

I'm confused by the use of 'private static final' for caches in Java. If the goal is to keep the cache consistent, why is it marked final if we can still add new users to it via a public method? Doesn't this defeat the purpose of the final keyword?

I am trying to organize my code by nesting a helper class. I have a class 'Engine' and a helper class 'Piston'. Since 'Piston' is only relevant to 'Engine', I want to nest it. However, when I try to declare a pointer to 'Piston' inside the 'Engine' private section, I get a compiler error saying 'Piston' is not defined. I've placed the class definition of 'Piston' below the member variable declaration. Do I need to forward declare the nested class inside the outer class, or should I move the nested class definition to the top of the class body? Also, if I define the methods of the nested class in a separate .cpp file, is that still considered proper encapsulation?

I'm working on a C++ project and want to hide the implementation details of my 'Database' class. I created a 'Connection' class that handles the low-level socket logic. I tried moving 'Connection' inside the private section of 'Database' so that no other part of the program can instantiate a 'Connection' object. But now I'm struggling with the implementation file. When I try to define the methods of 'Connection' in Database.cpp, the compiler doesn't recognize the scope. How do I correctly define methods for a private nested class in an external file while maintaining strict encapsulation?

I'm confused about how Java handles object references versus primitives. If I pass an object of a custom class to a method and change one of its fields using a setter, the change persists outside the method. However, if I pass an int and change it, it doesn't. Does this mean objects aren't encapsulated when passed to methods?

Why can a method modify the contents of an array passed to it, but it cannot change the value of a primitive variable passed to it? I thought Java was strictly pass-by-value. How does this relate to how memory is managed for arrays?

If I have a private array in a class and I provide a public getter method that returns that array, can a user of the class modify the elements of the array without using a setter? Isn't this a violation of encapsulation?

In Java, if I pass a StringBuilder to a method and call .append(), the original string is modified. But if I pass a String and try to change it, it doesn't work. Why is there a difference in how these two types are handled regarding state modification?

I am struggling to understand why passing an array to a function allows the function to change the array's elements. If Java passes everything by value, shouldn't it be passing a copy of the array instead of a reference to it?

How does Java's handling of array references impact the principle of encapsulation? Specifically, if a method receives an array as a parameter, it can change the internal state of that array. Is there a way to prevent this?

Why does changing a member of an object inside a method reflect in the original object, while changing a local primitive variable does not? I'm trying to understand the underlying mechanism of reference passing in Java.

If I return an array from a private field via a public method, I noticed that external classes can change the values inside that array. How can I return the data while still maintaining strict encapsulation?
Is it true that Java only passes the value of the reference when dealing with arrays? If so, why does modifying the array indices inside a method affect the array in the calling method?

I'm seeing a discrepancy: passing an integer to a method doesn't change the original, but passing an array does. Is this because arrays are treated as objects in Java, and how does this affect data hiding?

Can someone explain why the following happens: a method can sort an array passed to it and the caller sees the sorted array, but a method cannot increment an int passed to it and have the caller see the incremented value?

When we talk about encapsulation, we usually mean making fields private. But if those fields are arrays, and we provide a getter, the private modifier seems useless. Why is this the case and how is it fixed?
Does the fact that arrays are mutable objects in Java mean that they are inherently harder to encapsulate than primitive types?
I'm confused about the 'pass-by-value' rule in Java. If it's always pass-by-value, why can a method modify the elements of an array passed as an argument, but not the array reference itself?

If I pass an object to a method and the method assigns that parameter to null, the original object isn't null. But if the method changes a property of the object, the change stays. Why is the behavior different for the reference vs the object state?

Why is it that an array passed to a static method can be modified, whereas a primitive variable passed to the same method cannot be changed? I'm trying to understand the difference between stack and heap allocation here.
In the context of encapsulation, how do we prevent a method from modifying an array that is passed to it? Is there a way to pass a 'read-only' version of an array in Java?
If I have a class with a private array, and I return it through a getter, is the encapsulation broken? How does Java handle the reference to that array when it's passed around?
Why does the call to a sorting method change the original array passed into it, but a method that tries to reassign a primitive variable doesn't change the original value?

I am confused about reference types. If I pass an array to a method, it seems like I'm passing the actual array, not a copy. How does this differ from passing an integer, and what does this mean for data encapsulation?
Why does Java allow methods to modify the contents of an array passed as a parameter? I thought the whole point of encapsulation and scope was to prevent unintended side effects on variables.
If I pass a custom object to a function and modify its fields, the changes persist. If I pass an int, they don't. Why is the behavior for objects (and arrays) different from primitives in Java?
How can I ensure that an array returned by a getter cannot be modified by the caller? I'm worried that returning the reference violates the principle of encapsulation.
Explain the difference between passing a primitive and passing an array in Java. Why does the array's content change when modified in a method, but the primitive doesn't?
Does Java's pass-by-value mechanism apply to the reference of the array rather than the array itself? How does this explain why the array's elements can be changed inside a method?
I noticed that when I pass an array to a method, the method can change the values inside the array. Why doesn't this happen with a simple integer variable?
Is the ability to modify an array's elements within a method a flaw in Java's encapsulation, or is it a designed feature of how object references work?
Why is it that I can change the elements of an array passed to a method, but if I try to point that array to a new array inside the method, the change isn't reflected in the main method?
If encapsulation is about controlling access to data, why does Java allow any method that has a reference to an array to change its contents regardless of where the array was defined?
What is the technical reason why an array passed to a method is modified, but an int passed to a method is not? I'm trying to understand the difference between value types and reference types.
If I want to pass an array to a method but ensure the method cannot change any of the values, how should I do it? Does Java provide a way to encapsulate the array's contents during a method call?
Why does a method like Arrays.sort(myArray) modify the original array instead of returning a new sorted copy? How does this relate to how Java handles memory for arrays?

I am confused: if I pass an object to a method, the method can change the object's state. If I pass a primitive, it can't. Is this because the primitive is stored on the stack and the object on the heap?
How do I prevent 'leaking' the internal state of a class when returning an array from a getter method? I've heard this is a common encapsulation error in Java.
Why can a method change the values inside an array passed as an argument, but it cannot change the actual variable that holds the array reference?
In Java, why is it that modifying an object's field inside a method persists after the method returns, but changing a local variable passed as a parameter does not?
Can someone explain why arrays are treated differently than primitives when passed into methods? I'm trying to understand the concept of reference passing vs value passing.
If I have a private array in my class, is it truly encapsulated if I provide a public method that returns that array? What happens if the caller modifies the returned array?
Why does passing an array to a method allow that method to alter the original array's elements, while passing an int doesn't allow the method to alter the original int?
